text.title: nodejs-patterns Core Rules
text.summary: Seven testable rules for a layered TypeScript Express service. Each rule is enforceable by a script or code review.

rule.statement: Register middleware in this order inside createApp(): helmet → cors → express.json → express.urlencoded → compression → requestLogger → routes → errorHandler. The error handler MUST be the last app.use(...) call.
rule.rationale: Helmet/CORS must wrap responses before any handler runs. The error middleware only fires for downstream errors — placing it before routes makes it unreachable.

rule.statement: Export a pure createApp(): Express factory that returns the configured app without calling app.listen(). Listening happens only in server.ts.
rule.rationale: Tests must pump requests through createApp() without binding a port. Calling listen() inside the factory forces every test file to allocate a free port, blocking parallelism and producing flakes.

rule.statement: Resolve process.env exactly once, in config/env.ts, validated with Zod (or equivalent). All other modules import the frozen config object.
rule.rationale: Scattered process.env reads cannot be type-checked or stubbed in tests. A single validated boundary fails the process on startup if a required variable is missing, rather than at 3am inside a request handler.

rule.statement: Every thrown error extends a base AppError class carrying statusCode, code, and isOperational fields. Use Error.captureStackTrace in the base constructor.
rule.rationale: Distinguishing operational (expected: 404, 400, 401) from programmer errors lets the error handler reply safely to clients and lets the process crash on bugs so systemd can restart. captureStackTrace preserves call sites for debugging.

rule.statement: Every async handler is either wrapped with asyncHandler() or contains an explicit try/catch with next(error). No raw async route handlers without one of these.
rule.rationale: Unhandled promise rejections inside async handlers in Express 4 are not caught by the error middleware. Express 5 fixes this; while supporting 4 the wrapper is mandatory.

rule.statement: Log via the Pino logger (or app.log). Direct console.log/console.error calls are forbidden in non-bootstrap code. The requestLogger middleware emits one info line per finished request with requestId, method, url, statusCode, duration_ms.
rule.rationale: console.* outputs unstructured strings; production log aggregators expect JSON lines. Per-request structured lines enable trace correlation and SLO dashboards without re-parsing.

rule.statement: Install a SIGTERM/SIGINT handler that (a) stops accepting new connections, (b) drains active requests for ≤ 30s, (c) calls logger.flush() before process.exit().
rule.rationale: Pino transports buffer log lines; an unflushed shutdown loses the last seconds of logs — exactly the seconds that explain the crash. Graceful drain prevents truncated responses to clients.
